content.text: The Inception Score (IS) was the first widely adopted automatic metric for evaluating the quality and diversity of GAN-generated images. Before IS, GAN papers relied on human evaluation (costly and inconsistent) or domain-specific metrics that did not transfer across tasks. IS provided a single number that correlated roughly with human judgment of image quality for natural image generators, enabling systematic comparison across methods and training runs. The score uses a pretrained InceptionV3 classifier: good samples should be clearly recognisable as one specific class (high confidence, low entropy conditional distribution), and the collection of samples should cover many classes (high diversity, high entropy marginal distribution). IS is the exponentiated KL divergence between these two distributions. Despite its widespread use, IS has well-documented failure modes — it does not measure fidelity to the training distribution and can be gamed — so understanding what it measures and what it misses is essential for using it correctly.

Given a set of generated images, IS is computed using the InceptionV3 classifier. For each image x, let p(y|x) be the conditional label distribution output by InceptionV3 (a 1000-dimensional softmax). Let p(y) = E_x[p(y|x)] be the marginal distribution over labels (the average predicted class distribution across all generated images).

The KL divergence D_KL(p(y|x) || p(y)) is high when p(y|x) is very peaked (one class strongly predicted) and p(y) is spread out (many classes represented across the sample set). IS is the exponential of the expected KL, so higher IS is better. A perfect generator would produce images that are each clearly recognisable as one class (sharp p(y|x)) while collectively covering all classes uniformly (uniform p(y)). The theoretical maximum IS on a 1000-class classifier is e^log(1000) = 1000, but in practice no GAN comes close — state-of-the-art models score in the 200–300 range on ImageNet.

: measured by the entropy of p(y|x). Low entropy means the classifier is confident — the image clearly resembles one class. Blurry or unrecognisable images produce high entropy (uncertain classification across all classes).

: measured by the entropy of p(y). High entropy means many different classes appear in the generated set. A mode-collapsed generator that produces only one class would have p(y) concentrated on one class, giving low entropy and low IS.
Formally: IS = exp(E[D_KL(p(y|x) || p(y))]) = exp(H(p(y)) - E[H(p(y|x))]). It is the exponentiated difference between marginal entropy (diversity) and expected conditional entropy (quality).

IS relies on InceptionV3 pretrained on ImageNet. This creates a critical dependency: IS measures quality and diversity as perceived by InceptionV3, not as humans perceive them. InceptionV3 is good at recognising 1000 specific ImageNet classes, but this means:

IS only works well for natural image datasets with ImageNet-like content (animals, objects, scenes). For medical images, satellite imagery, or abstract art, IS is meaningless — InceptionV3 has not learned relevant features for those domains.

IS does not compare to the real data distribution. It only measures properties of the generated images themselves. A generator producing beautiful images that share no overlap with the training distribution would score well on IS but fail on FID.

IS requires images at InceptionV3's expected input size. Images must be resized to 299×299 before scoring. This can hurt IS for low-resolution generators (upsampling from 64×64 to 299×299 introduces artifacts that affect the classifier).

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import torchvision.models as models
import torchvision.transforms as T
import numpy as np
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

class InceptionScoreEvaluator:
    """
    Computes Inception Score with mean and standard deviation across splits.
    Standard protocol: 50,000 samples, 10 splits.
    """
    def __init__(self, device='cuda', n_splits=10, batch_size=64):
        self.device = device
        self.n_splits = n_splits
        self.batch_size = batch_size

        # Load InceptionV3 pretrained on ImageNet
        self.inception = models.inception_v3(
            weights=models.Inception_V3_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1,
            transform_input=False  # we normalise manually
        )
        self.inception.eval()
        # Use only the features up to the auxiliary classifier
        # Remove final FC layer — we want logits (1000,)
        self.inception = self.inception.to(device)
        for p in self.inception.parameters():
            p.requires_grad_(False)

        # InceptionV3 expects [0, 1] normalised, then centre-cropped to 299x299
        self.transform = T.Compose([
            T.Resize(299),
            T.CenterCrop(299),
            T.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                        std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
        ])

    def get_predictions(self, images):
        """
        images: tensor (N, 3, H, W) in [-1, 1] (standard GAN output range)
        Returns: p(y|x) softmax predictions, shape (N, 1000)
        """
        # Convert [-1, 1] to [0, 1]
        images = (images + 1) / 2.0
        preds = []
        dataset = TensorDataset(images)
        loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=self.batch_size)

        for (batch,) in loader:
            batch = self.transform(batch.to(self.device))
            with torch.no_grad():
                logits = self.inception(batch)
                # inception_v3 returns InceptionOutputs namedtuple in training mode
                if hasattr(logits, 'logits'):
                    logits = logits.logits
            preds.append(F.softmax(logits, dim=1).cpu())

        return torch.cat(preds, dim=0)  # (N, 1000)

    def compute(self, images):
        """
        Compute IS mean and std over n_splits partitions.
        Protocol: n_splits=10, N >= 50,000 recommended.
        """
        preds = self.get_predictions(images)  # (N, 1000)
        N = preds.shape[0]
        split_size = N // self.n_splits

        is_scores = []
        for i in range(self.n_splits):
            split = preds[i * split_size : (i + 1) * split_size]  # (split_size, 1000)

            # Marginal distribution: average over samples in this split
            py = split.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)  # (1, 1000)

            # KL divergence: sum over classes, average over samples
            kl = (split * (split.log() - py.log())).sum(dim=1)  # (split_size,)
            is_scores.append(kl.mean().exp().item())

        return float(np.mean(is_scores)), float(np.std(is_scores))


# Usage:
# evaluator = InceptionScoreEvaluator(device='cuda')
# G.eval()
# with torch.no_grad():
#     z = torch.randn(50000, 100)
#     images = torch.cat([G(z[i:i+64]) for i in range(0, 50000, 64)])
# mean_is, std_is = evaluator.compute(images)
# print(f"IS: {mean_is:.2f} ± {std_is:.2f}")
IS has several well-documented problems that limit its reliability as a standalone metric:
Does not compare to real data

: IS measures generated images only. A generator that produces realistic adversarial examples — images that fool InceptionV3 into maximum confidence on one class, but look like noise to humans — can achieve high IS. Barratt & Sharma (2018) showed IS is easily gamed.

: IS measures inter-class diversity (how many classes appear) but not intra-class diversity (how many different dog breeds, or dog poses, or dog backgrounds appear). A generator producing one identical golden retriever for every "dog" sample would score the same IS as a generator producing thousands of diverse dogs.
: InceptionV3 biases towards ImageNet-recognisable objects. Generators producing realistic but non-ImageNet content (abstract art, medical images, satellite maps) score poorly or randomly.

: IS changes when images are upsampled to 299×299. The upsampling method (bilinear, bicubic, nearest) affects the score. This makes IS unreliable for comparing generators trained at different resolutions.
High variance with small sample size
: IS requires at least 10,000–50,000 samples for reliable estimates. With fewer samples, split-level variance is high and comparisons between methods become unreliable.

Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) addresses the main weakness of IS by comparing the real and generated distributions, not just evaluating generated images in isolation. FID uses InceptionV3 as a feature extractor (not a classifier), takes features from the pool_3 layer (a 2048-dimensional representation), and measures the Fréchet distance between the Gaussian approximations of the real and generated feature distributions:
where (μ_r, Σ_r) and (μ_g, Σ_g) are the mean and covariance of InceptionV3 pool_3 features for real and generated images respectively. Lower FID is better.
No (both real and generated from same dist)
: the current standard. Compares feature distributions. Lower is better. Used in nearly all modern GAN papers.

: decomposes generation quality into precision (fraction of generated images in the real distribution — quality) and recall (fraction of real images covered by generated distribution — diversity). Better at separating quality from coverage than IS.
: for text-to-image models, cosine similarity between CLIP image embeddings and CLIP text embeddings of the prompt. Does not require real images and generalises across domains.
: perceptual similarity between paired images using deep feature distances. Requires reference images — useful for conditional models (super-resolution, Pix2Pix) but not unconditional generation.

from torchmetrics.image.fid import FrechetInceptionDistance
from torchmetrics.image.inception import InceptionScore
import torch

def evaluate_gan_metrics(G, real_loader, z_dim=100, n_samples=50000, device='cuda'):
    """
    Compute IS and FID for a GAN generator.
    Requires torchmetrics: pip install torchmetrics[image]
    """
    G.eval()

    # torchmetrics handles feature extraction internally
    fid_metric = FrechetInceptionDistance(normalize=True).to(device)
    is_metric  = InceptionScore(normalize=True).to(device)

    # Update FID with real images
    print("Computing real image features for FID...")
    for real_batch, _ in real_loader:
        real_batch = real_batch.to(device)
        # torchmetrics expects [0, 1] with normalize=True, or uint8 without
        real_01 = (real_batch * 0.5 + 0.5).clamp(0, 1)  # [-1,1] → [0,1]
        fid_metric.update(real_01, real=True)

    # Generate samples and update both metrics
    print(f"Generating {n_samples} samples...")
    batch_size = 64
    n_generated = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        while n_generated < n_samples:
            this_batch = min(batch_size, n_samples - n_generated)
            z = torch.randn(this_batch, z_dim, device=device)
            fake = G(z)
            fake_01 = (fake * 0.5 + 0.5).clamp(0, 1)

            fid_metric.update(fake_01, real=False)
            is_metric.update(fake_01)
            n_generated += this_batch

    fid_score = fid_metric.compute().item()
    is_mean, is_std = is_metric.compute()

    return {
        'FID': fid_score,                # lower is better
        'IS_mean': is_mean.item(),       # higher is better
        'IS_std': is_std.item()
    }


# Example output interpretation:
# FID=3.8 (excellent), IS=7.6±0.1 → high quality and diverse
# FID=50.0 (moderate), IS=7.5±0.2 → diverse but different distribution
# FID=8.0 (good), IS=4.0±0.1 → low diversity (mode collapse)
IS can be used as a training signal during evaluation checkpoints. Typical trajectories:
: both quality and diversity are improving — healthy training.
IS plateau followed by sharp drop
: generator converged to limited modes (mode collapse), diversity component collapsed.
: mode cycling — generator repeatedly changing which modes it covers without converging. Check D/G loss balance and apply label smoothing or WGAN-GP.

: generator produces recognisable images from many classes but those images do not match the real distribution. Often indicates the generator has memorised a different set of features than what's in the training data.
Using IS as the sole evaluation metric.
Always report FID alongside IS. IS does not compare to real data, so it can be high for a generator that looks nothing like the training set. FID catches this.
Computing IS on fewer than 10,000 samples.
Split-level IS estimates with small N have very high variance. IS reported with < 5000 samples per split is unreliable. Use at least 50,000 samples divided into 10 splits of 5,000 each.

IS uses InceptionV3 pretrained on ImageNet. For medical images, satellite imagery, or text rendering, IS values are meaningless — InceptionV3 has not learned relevant features. Use domain-specific classifiers or FID with domain-appropriate features.

IS changes with resolution because upsampling artifacts affect InceptionV3's predictions differently. Only compare IS between models trained and evaluated at the same resolution with the same upsampling method.
Always report IS as mean ± std over 10 splits of 50,000 samples.
The split-level standard deviation quantifies IS estimation uncertainty. Comparisons without error bars are not meaningful.
Report FID alongside IS for all papers.
FID is the stronger metric because it compares to real data. IS provides a complementary perspective on diversity but does not replace FID.
Use IS for quick training monitoring, FID for final evaluation.

IS computation is fast (no real image features required) and useful for tracking progress every N epochs. FID requires real image features and is more expensive but more reliable for final comparison.
For conditional generators, compute class-conditional IS.
Split generated samples by class and compute IS within each class (intra-class diversity). This reveals whether the generator is diverse within classes, not just across classes.

Think of IS as a test with two parts: image clarity and portfolio diversity. The clarity test asks: does each image clearly look like one specific thing? (a cat, a car, a landscape — not "some blur that could be anything"). The diversity test asks: does your full set of images cover many different things, or do they all look like the same kind of thing? A perfect score requires each image to be unmistakably one thing, while the overall collection spans everything. IS combines these two into one number, which is why a generator suffering mode collapse (all images are dogs) or producing blurry outputs (no image is clearly anything) both score poorly. The limitation is that IS does not check whether your images look anything like the real photographs — it only checks whether InceptionV3 recognises them confidently and whether you cover many classes.

IS = exp(E[D_KL(p(y|x) || p(y))]). High IS requires low conditional entropy (each image clearly one class) and high marginal entropy (many classes represented). It is higher for more realistic and more diverse generators.

IS does not compare to real data. A generator producing adversarial examples that fool InceptionV3 at maximum confidence, but that look like noise to humans, can achieve near-perfect IS. Never use IS as the only metric.
IS only works for ImageNet-like content. For any other domain, use FID with a domain-appropriate feature extractor or a domain-specific downstream metric.
Report IS as mean ± std over 10 splits of ≥ 50,000 samples. Fewer samples produce unreliable estimates. The standard deviation quantifies estimation uncertainty.
FID is the preferred primary metric. It compares real and generated feature distributions, detects mode collapse more reliably, and correlates better with human judgment than IS.

Precision/Recall decomposes what IS conflates: precision measures quality (are generated images realistic?), recall measures coverage (are all real distribution modes covered?). Use them together for a complete picture.
